feed.title: SkillsLLM
feed.subtitle: AI tooling news and guides: SkillsLLM, Desktop Commander, Model Context Protocol, FastMCP, ClaudePluginHub, MCP Servers blog, Claude Skills Hub, OpenRouter, LiteLLM (blog + releases), Glama (blog, MCP servers, release notes), LobeHub (changelog + blog), and AI Skill Market

entry.title: Merchant of Record, Explained: Paddle vs. Stripe
entry.content: Stripe processes payments; Paddle becomes the legal seller. For a solo founder selling digital products across borders, that distinction decides who handles VAT, GST, refunds, and chargebacks — you or them.

entry.title: Why Stripe Won't Let You Take Payments Yet
entry.content: You created a Stripe account, sent a payment link, and hit 'your account cannot currently make live charges.' Here's what actually activates live mode — and the shortest path from zero to a real first dollar.

entry.title: Pillar Pages and Content Clusters for AI Citation
entry.content: Digital Applied found 86% of AI citations come from sites with 5+ interlinked pieces on one topic. Yext found a bidirectionally-linked cluster gets cited 2.7x more than an isolated page. AI crawlers read internal links as a map — here's how to structure content as one knowledge node.

entry.title: Ten Schema Tags That Get You Cited by AI
entry.content: Schema markup alone does nothing — Ahrefs tested 1,885 pages and adding it with no content change produced no citation lift. But Organization and FAQPage JSON-LD on top of real content earn 2.7x more AI citations. Here's the code and the deployment checklist.

entry.title: The Reddit Playbook for Solo Founders Who Hate Marketing
entry.content: Reddit is Perplexity's #1 source at 46.7% of top citations and #1 in Google AI Overviews at 21%. One genuinely useful 30-minute answer keeps getting pulled into AI responses indefinitely. Here's the minimal-effort playbook — and why this window won't stay open.

entry.title: Why AI Engines Cite Reddit Over Your Homepage
entry.content: AI engines trust what others say about you far more than what you say about yourself. Muck Rack found 82% of AI citations come from earned media, under 6% from brand-owned pages. The real work of GEO isn't polishing your copy — it's getting mentioned elsewhere.

entry.title: Search Traffic Forked: SEO and GEO Are Different Games
entry.content: Search just split into two paths: the ranked blue-link list and the AI-generated answer that quotes a few sources nobody clicks. SEO races for rank; GEO races to be the source the AI draws from. Here's why the distinction reorders how solo founders get found.

entry.title: The Compliance Deadline That Costs $25,000 If You Miss It
entry.content: Most 'form a US LLC' guides stop at the EIN. The part they skip can cost $25,000: Form 5472, required annually from foreign-owned LLCs even at zero revenue. Plus the BOI rule that changed in 2025 and the state report you still owe.

entry.title: Getting an EIN Without a Social Security Number
entry.content: No SSN? You can still get an EIN by phone or fax — the widely-repeated claim that the IRS killed the fax channel is wrong. Here's the SS-4 prep that keeps you from getting rejected: Line 7b, Line 11, Line 15, and the Responsible Party rule.

entry.title: Wyoming vs. Delaware vs. California for a Solo LLC
entry.content: Why an LLC beats a C-Corp for most solo founders, and which state to form in. Wyoming's $60-a-year default, Delaware's investor pull, and California's $800 franchise-tax trap — plus the registered-agent requirement nobody explains until you're stuck.

entry.title: Give Your AI Agent Legs: Control It From Your Phone
entry.content: The real payoff of a personal mesh VPN: run an AI coding agent on a home machine and drive it from your phone while the task keeps running. Here's the latency math, the security details that matter, and the one hard limit to plan around.

entry.title: Access Your Home Server From Anywhere, No Open Ports
entry.content: Tailscale turns every device you own into one private network — no public IP, no firewall rules, no forwarded ports. Here's the connection model that makes it work, and the one thing that quietly leaves you stuck on relay latency.

entry.title: Build a Waitlist Page in 30 Minutes, Zero Servers
entry.content: A concrete 6-step path from empty folder to a live email-capture page — frontend, backend, and database — deployed worldwide on Cloudflare for $0, before a single line of the real product exists. The fastest way to test if anyone wants the thing.

entry.title: Deploy Global Infrastructure for Free on Cloudflare
entry.content: Cloudflare Workers and Pages replace 'rent a VPS and configure nginx' with 'push code, it runs in 335 cities.' Here's why serverless-at-the-edge is a different model, where the free tier ceiling actually sits, and how Pages and Workers are the same primitive.

entry.title: Keeping Your Free Oracle VPS From Getting Reclaimed
entry.content: Oracle's free tier has one real string attached: it reclaims idle instances. Here's what 'idle' actually means for a dev box, the keep-alive pattern that works, why community scripts are a fragile bet, and the fix that ends the problem for good.

entry.title: Oracle's Always-Free Tier: The Server Everyone Forgets
entry.content: Oracle Cloud quietly gives away a permanent 4-core, 24GB-RAM ARM server that would cost $80-100/month on AWS. Here's why signups get rejected, the region choice you can't undo, and how to actually get the machine.

entry.title: Your First US Bank Card Without Setting Foot in America
entry.content: The four-piece kit a non-resident founder needs before a US bank says yes: ITIN, a CMRA-clean address, a US SIM, and a Wise account, plus the card application.

entry.title: The ITIN Path: A US Tax ID Without a US Address
entry.content: What an ITIN is, why a Certifying Acceptance Agent beats mailing your passport to the IRS, the 8-12 week timeline, and why it's the first US-entity domino.

entry.title: Ship a Static Site With Nothing but Git Push
entry.content: Why GitHub Pages beats Vercel, Netlify, and a VPS for a solo founder's first landing page: zero cost, no third-party login, and the deploy is a git push.

entry.title: Domain Name to Live DNS in Twenty Minutes
entry.content: Registrar tradeoffs, why to route DNS through Cloudflare, the DNSSEC gotcha that takes domains offline, and the orange-vs-grey cloud rule for GitHub Pages.

entry.title: The Questions Every Teacher Asks Before Signing Up
entry.content: Eligibility, verification, cost, data privacy, and what happens if you change schools — the practical FAQ for ChatGPT for Teachers, and a look back at the series.

entry.title: What Happens When the Free Period Ends in 2027
entry.content: ChatGPT for Teachers is free only through June 2027. OpenAI hasn't said what comes next. Here's what's known, what's speculation, and what to plan for regardless.

entry.title: No, Students Don't Get Free ChatGPT Too
entry.content: ChatGPT for Teachers is a teacher-and-staff offer, not a student one — and several big districts ban student ChatGPT outright. Untangling the confusion.

entry.title: Choosing a Pilot: A Decision Guide for Districts
entry.content: ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, or Copilot for your district AI pilot? A practical framework built on the stack you already run, real per-seat prices, and priorities.

entry.title: Building the Skill a Free Chatbot Can't Replace
entry.content: A free, general teacher assistant is genuinely useful — and structurally incapable of three things a purpose-built skill does. Here's the line between them.

entry.title: Where AI Tutoring Skills Fit Between the Giants
entry.content: ChatGPT for Teachers and Claude for Teachers are both teacher-facing productivity tools. The student-facing Socratic tutor is a niche neither giant is actually filling.

entry.title: What Claude Skill Builders Can Learn From OpenAI
entry.content: OpenAI's education playbook — narrow vertical, distribution through a trusted intermediary, free to build the habit — is a GTM lesson for anyone building a Claude Code skill.

entry.title: The Squeeze on Third-Party Classroom AI Tools
entry.content: Paid lesson-planning and differentiation tools now compete with something free from the model vendor itself. What the squeeze looks like, and which tools escape it.

entry.title: What a Free Vertical AI Product Means for EdTech
entry.content: When the model vendor ships a free, polished, education-specific product directly to teachers, the ecosystem built on its general-purpose API has to rethink what it's for.

entry.title: Piloting ChatGPT for Teachers District-Wide
entry.content: A free tool still costs you a rollout. How to pilot ChatGPT for Teachers across a district — cohort selection, a real feedback loop, and the scale-or-stop decision.

entry.title: Role-Based Access Control for School Workspaces
entry.content: Who should be an admin, who should be a member, and the RBAC misconfigurations that quietly hand a whole district's AI workspace to the wrong people. A practical guide for K-12 IT.

entry.title: Reading the Student Data Privacy Agreement
entry.content: OpenAI names itself a FERPA 'School Official' and says student data stays under the school's control. Here's what those terms actually commit — and what a privacy officer should still ask.

entry.title: SSO, MFA, and the District IT Rollout Plan
entry.content: The mechanics of a managed ChatGPT for Teachers deployment: claim the domain, wire up SAML SSO, enforce MFA, and onboard teachers in phases without flooding the help desk.

entry.title: A Procurement Checklist for ChatGPT for Teachers
entry.content: It's free, so districts skip procurement. That's the mistake. Here's the checklist an IT director should run before ChatGPT for Teachers reaches a single classroom.

entry.title: When Voice Mode Beats Typing in the Classroom
entry.content: Voice mode isn't a gimmick for teachers. It wins in the exact moments a classroom leaves your hands full and your keyboard out of reach. Here's when to talk instead of type.

entry.title: Image Generation for Worksheets and Slides
entry.content: How to use ChatGPT for Teachers' image generation for worksheets, slides, and classroom visuals — and why every AI-generated diagram needs an accuracy check first.

entry.title: Differentiation and IEP Support, Carefully Done
entry.content: How to use ChatGPT for Teachers to draft differentiated materials and IEP-aligned supports — and exactly where FERPA caution around student-identifying data has to stop you.

entry.title: Writing Parent Emails Without the Dread
entry.content: How ChatGPT for Teachers helps draft difficult parent emails — setting tone, structuring hard news, and translating — with the rule to always personalize and review first.

entry.title: Turning File Uploads Into Faster Grading Prep
entry.content: How file uploads in ChatGPT for Teachers speed up rubric-building and feedback drafting — without letting AI grade for you or auto-send anything to students.

entry.title: Co-Planning Lessons in Shared Projects
entry.content: How ChatGPT for Teachers' shared projects turn department co-planning from a Friday scramble into a durable, shared workspace. A realistic PLC workflow.

entry.title: Building a Custom GPT for Your Department
entry.content: A custom GPT turns a good prompt into a reusable tool your whole department shares. Here's how to build a rubric-grader or differentiation GPT that actually gets used.

entry.title: The Four-Way AI Vendor Race for the Classroom
entry.content: OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Microsoft are all racing for the same classrooms — with four different strategies. Here's how the board actually looks.

entry.title: Why OpenAI Chose Free Over a Freemium Tier
entry.content: OpenAI could have shipped a discounted teacher tier. It shipped a fully free one through June 2027. The go-to-market logic behind that choice is worth reading.

entry.title: Context Windows, Compared for Classroom Use
entry.content: Token context sounds abstract until you try uploading a whole class set of essays. Here's what 400K vs 1M–2M tokens actually means for a teacher's day.

entry.title: The Real Price of "Free": A Cost Comparison
entry.content: ChatGPT for Teachers is free per seat — but adoption isn't. Here's the true cost of rolling it out versus paid Gemini, Copilot, and Claude for schools.

entry.title: ChatGPT for Teachers vs Microsoft Copilot in Class
entry.content: Same GPT-5.1 engine, different chassis. A free standalone workspace versus an Office-native paid tier — and why the model isn't what decides this one.

entry.title: Gemini for Education: The Google Gravity Case
entry.content: For a Google-shop district, the AI question isn't which model is smarter. It's whether Workspace gravity and NotebookLM beat a free standalone ChatGPT.

entry.title: Claude for Education: The Other Free Offer
entry.content: OpenAI isn't the only company giving teachers AI for free. Anthropic's education play — a free K-12 offer plus a paid, partner-rich tier — is the quieter alternative.

entry.title: The Conflict-of-Interest Question in AI Training
entry.content: AI vendors are funding the union that trains teachers to use their tools. That's a real conflict worth naming — and the safeguards deserve a fair hearing too.

entry.title: Free for Teachers, Banned for Students: The Split
entry.content: OpenAI is courting teachers with a free workspace while districts ban ChatGPT for students. Is that a contradiction — or two different problems wearing one name?

entry.title: Oregon Teachers Are Calling AI Deals a "Grift"
entry.content: An Oregon NEA rank-and-file caucus wants an end to what it calls 'AI grift.' Here's what that grassroots pushback represents — and what it doesn't, within a mixed union landscape.

entry.title: What the MIT Cognitive Engagement Study Found
entry.content: An MIT study found students who used ChatGPT to write essays showed weaker cognitive engagement. Here's what that implies for student use — and why teacher use is a different case.

entry.title: Baltimore, LA, NYC, Seattle: Why They Banned ChatGPT
entry.content: Major districts banned ChatGPT over integrity and quality fears. Now OpenAI is courting those same districts with free teacher accounts. Are the two positions contradictory?

entry.title: Why Detection Tools Miss 1 in 5 AI Essays
entry.content: AI-writing detectors run roughly 80-90% accurate — and most don't publish rates at all. Here's why that's not good enough to be a teacher's primary defense.

entry.title: The Cheating Numbers Behind ChatGPT for Teachers
entry.content: A Study.com survey found over a quarter of teachers have caught a student cheating with ChatGPT. Here's what that number actually measures — and what it doesn't.

entry.title: Can a Course Make 400,000 Teachers AI-Literate
entry.content: The AFT's academy promises to train 400,000 teachers on AI in five years. But scale and depth pull against each other — can standardized training build real literacy?

entry.title: ChatGPT Foundations for Teachers, Reviewed
entry.content: A fair critical review of OpenAI's free teacher course — what it teaches well, and the gaps around AI grading, hallucination, and detection it glosses over.

entry.title: Inside the AI Skills Jam for K-12 Educators
entry.content: OpenAI's AI Skills Jam took the teacher training on the road — in-person workshops across US cities that drew 1,600+ educators. Here's what the format looks like.

entry.title: What OpenAI's Free Teacher AI Course Covers
entry.content: OpenAI Academy runs two free courses for K-12 teachers: AI Foundations and ChatGPT Foundations for Teachers. Here's the actual syllabus, section by section.

entry.title: Three Rivals, One Classroom: AFT's AI Coalition
entry.content: OpenAI, Microsoft, and Anthropic compete everywhere else — but co-funded the same $23M union program to train 400,000 teachers. Here's why.

entry.content: Write intentional, reviewable code with clear behavior and controlled complexity.

entry.title: iajus-pt

Inclui acórdãos dos tribunais superiores e das Relações via DGSI, legislação do Diário da República com ELI e três skills pt-PT para pesquisa multimodal, consulta de normas vigentes e estado atual do corpus. Instala skills e servidor MCP juntos, com OAuth 2.1 por defeito e chave ik_* apenas como alternativa privada.

entry.title: anafpy-workflows
entry.content: ANAF workflow skills for Claude Cowork: file an e-Transport declaration and get a UIT, author/validate/sign a tax declaration, and summarize personal income from the SPV mailbox. These skills drive the anafpy MCP server's tools, so the anafpy connector must also be configured (see the anafpy-setup plugin).

entry.title: anafpy-setup
entry.content: Install and configure anafpy on this computer, then connect it to Claude. Probes what is already in place, installs what is missing, and repairs a broken setup.

entry.title: googlecloud-plugin
entry.content: A full Google Cloud delivery team for your coding agent: vendor-objective solution designer, GCP architect, agentic-systems architect (ADK / Agent Runtime / MCP / A2A / AP2), plus security, SRE, and QA — wired into a design-first, security-first delivery gate. Eleven service skills (Cloud Run, GKE, IAM, BigQuery, Cloud Storage, Vertex AI, networking, observability), MCP server setup, and a self-validating research pipeline that keeps every reference current.

entry.title: code-gauntlet
entry.content: Adversarial multi-agent code review. Your PR runs a gauntlet of up to seven concern-specialized agents, then survives deterministic verification, skeptical validation, and a blind challenge before anything reaches you. Prompt injection defense and flexible delivery (PR/MR comments, markdown, chat). Supports GitHub and GitLab. Formerly deep-review.

entry.title: no-ai-slop
entry.content: Deterministic AI-slop guard: a PreToolUse hook greps content headed to Notion, Slack, Gmail, Artifacts, and prose .md files against a high-precision banned-phrase list and blocks it for rewrite before it is ever published. Ships the upstream no-ai-slop skill as the manual /command for structural patterns and audits.

entry.title: agent-stdlib

entry.title: ash
entry.content: ASH engineering skills: 38 guardrail skills in a four-layer architecture (hooks, norms, hubs, leaves), distilled from cross-repo lessons learned and committee-ratified. Includes the Layer 0 hooks (push gate, skill fire log, destructive-SQL warn, session recon) and the always-on norms injected at session start.

entry.title: cross-dex-trade

Lists tokens and AMM pairs, quotes buy / buy-exact / sell / deposit / withdraw routes, and submits slippage-bounded router transactions. Optionally dispatched through an OpenClaw agent. Bundles a Node.js trader, SOUL.md template, self-test, and credential-handling rules.

entry.title: scd-feature-specs
entry.content: Purely documentary per-feature spec workflow: the downstream suite of scd-project-docs. Consumes the kickoff socle (prd/stack/adr/CLAUDE.md) and produces, for one or several features, the documents development will build on — spec → plan → tasks — then attests they are ready for optimal implementation. Seven user-invoked phases (kickoff, specify, clarify, plan, tasks, analyze, premortem) plus a status dashboard, with EARS acceptance criteria, one-question-at-a-time interview, plan-mode planning and Kiro-style backreferences. Tasks are grouped into review lots (Rn): vertical slices sized so that a human can actually review each one once implemented — a perfectly traced contract shipped as one block yields a review nobody really does. Blocking criteria are qualitative (single subject, vertical slice, understandable alone); the numeric thresholds (~400 diff lines, ~7 concepts, ~60 min) are advisory split signals, since they are transposed from code-inspection studies and the budget is a documentary estimate, not a measurement. The analyze gate runs 14 read-only checks (traceability, EARS conformance, verifiable verbs over adjectives, technology-agnostic spec, EXCLUDED scope, socle coherence, slice verticality/cohesion/sizing) and returns a Critical/Major/Minor verdict. The optional premortem phase then hardens a conformant contract adversarially: three subagents — a facilitator projects the shipped feature failing and traces the cause back into the contract (broad product + documentary lenses), a validator triages the risks (rejecting speculation, already-covered, scope creep and style), and an applier writes only the human-approved remediations into spec/plan/tasks (new EARS criterion, FR, EXCLUDED item, task, plan note or ADR candidate), after which analyze is re-run since the contract changed. Everything touching code is OUT of scope — neither writing it nor reviewing it afterwards; no test is ever executed here: the split makes the downstream review possible, it does not conduct it. Every command resolves its target feature from disk, so state survives /clear with no state file to drift, and documenting several features in parallel is safe. Ships an ADR-immutability PreToolUse hook, a format PostToolUse hook, two read-only auditor subagents with disjoint mandates (contract and slicing) plus a three-agent premortem crew (facilitator, validator, applier), OpenSpec brownfield deltas, a propose-only spec-drift maintenance loop and a Gherkin complement. Human decides the what; Claude drafts against templates, interviews to fill gaps, adversarially audits both the contract and its slicing, and — only with explicit human approval — hardens the contract against projected failure.

entry.title: scd-implement
entry.content: Workflow dynamique d'implémentation, la suite de scd-feature-specs. Consomme le contrat documentaire d'une feature (specs/NNN-feature/{spec,plan,tasks}.md, verdict analyze au vert) et exécute, pour UN lot de review Rn à la fois, un dynamic workflow (script JS orchestrant des subagents dédiés en arrière-plan) qui honore le MODE DE VÉRIFICATION déclaré par le lot (_vérif :_ ∈ TDD par défaut, test-after, check ou inhérent — le TDD n'est plus la loi unique). Le préambule (branche → rebase → brief) et le final (review → triage → apply → record → PR) sont invariants ; seul le segment de vérification varie : TDD → tests au rouge, validés (une SHALL EARS = un test nommé, couverture, cas limites, conventions du rubric), puis impl jusqu'au vert sans jamais toucher aux tests (check git-diff déterministe, pas un hook) ; test-after → impl d'abord puis tests écrits après (au vert), toujours validés ; check → vérification observable dédiée par l'agent verifier en contexte frais ; inhérent → le critère d'acceptation de l'impl EST la preuve (CI vert, terraform converge), ré-exécuté par le verifier. Ce qu'un agent ne peut pas constater (rendu visuel, effet externe) part en humanCheckRequired, remonté en checklist dans la PR. Puis review du code sur six dimensions (architecture, propreté, conventions, couverture, sécurité, gestion d'erreur) sur TOUS les modes, triage sceptique adversarial des findings (chaque finding reproduit avant d'être retenu, style et scope creep rejetés, on ne corrige que ce qui touche la correction ou une exigence), application des seuls findings validés et re-vérification selon le mode, et enfin publication d'une PR « ready for review » (une PR par lot : branche de lot dédiée, description structurée — mode, FR/SHALL livrés, tests ou preuve observable, checklist humaine, findings, preuve). Producteur (implémenteur) et vérificateurs (reviewer, verifier) ne partagent jamais de contexte — le second regard en contexte frais tue le self-preferential bias. La porte de vérif exige une preuve mécanique — sortie 0 failed (modes-test) ou preuve observable capturée (check/inhérent) — jamais une affirmation. Défaut TDD si le marqueur _vérif :_ est absent : rétro-compatible avec tout tasks.md existant. Avant tout travail, le workflow crée TOUJOURS une branche dédiée impl/<slug>-<lot> à partir de la base à jour (git fetch) ; il exige un arbre de travail propre et s'arrête proprement sinon. La base est résolue de façon déterministe par /scd-implement:run : --base explicite, sinon auto-stacking (un lot qui dépend d'un lot Rk non encore mergé s'empile sur impl/<slug>-Rk pour le fork ET la PR, sans rejouer son diff), sinon branche par défaut du repo. Trois garde-fous déterministes : pr-author refuse une PR dont la tête descend d'une PR ouverte visant la même base (anti-chevauchement), l'orchestrateur bloque si les commits atterrissent hors de la branche du lot, et l'anti-orphelinage des PR empilées ferme la faille du stacking — si un humain merge une PR empilée dont la base est encore une branche de lot intermédiaire, GitHub fusionne le code dans ce cul-de-sac (jamais dans la branche par défaut) et le code devient orphelin (PR MERGED, absente de main). Prévention : pr-author ouvre toute PR empilée (base ≠ défaut) en DRAFT (barrière naturelle : un draft ne se merge pas sans passage ready explicite), pose les labels stacked + needs-sync et préfixe la description d'un bloc « ne pas merger directement — synchronise d'abord ». Détection : /scd-implement:status classe chaque PR de lot en OK, ⚠️ DANGEREUX (empilé, dépendance mergée → merger orphelinerait), ⚠️ EMPILÉ EN ATTENTE (dépendance pas encore mergée) ou 🔴 ORPHELIN (déjà mergé hors du défaut, code absent de main), sur le signal contenu robuste au squash (Tn cochés dans origin/défaut:tasks.md) corroboré par git merge-base --is-ancestor. Remédiation : /scd-implement:sync retargete/rebase/passe ready la PR dangereuse (retire needs-sync), et /scd-implement:reland rapatrie un orphelin (nouvelle branche depuis le défaut → cherry-pick des commits propres du lot → nouvelle PR ready → commentaire sur l'orpheline), jamais de résolution de conflit automatique. Le rebase est une brique nommée et déterministe (agent rebaser, modèle haiku) : transplant exact des commits propres du lot via git rebase --onto (robuste au merge/squash/rebase de la dépendance), idempotent, jamais de résolution de conflit automatique, jamais de --force sec (uniquement --force-with-lease). Deux déclencheurs : préventif (phase Rebase du workflow, repose la branche sur la base à jour avant d'écrire — no-op sur une branche fraîche) et curatif (commande /scd-implement:sync : re-rebase les PR de lot ouvertes quand leur dépendance vient d'être mergée — le cas « R1 mergé → rebase R2 » — et retargete la base de la PR). /scd-implement:status signale la dérive (PR ouverte dont la dépendance est mergée mais la branche non rebasée). Parallélisme réel par worktree git (depuis 0.5.0) : un mode worktree optionnel isole l'exécution d'un lot dans un checkout git dédié (git worktree add hors de l'arbre suivi, chemin absolu propagé à chaque agent aval qui roote alors toutes ses opérations dessus — git -C, chemins absolus, cwd de test = worktree). Il lève le blocage arbre-sale et permet à plusieurs lots de tourner sans se disputer le HEAD/arbre unique de la session (couche 1, collision d'exécution). La commande /scd-implement:run-parallel exploite ce mode : elle résout plusieurs lots, calcule la co-parallélisabilité (fichiers disjoints via la ligne Fichiers: ET aucune dépendance mutuelle non mergée → lots co-lançables ; sinon sérialisés/empilés en chaîne --base — couche 2, conflit de contenu, que l'isolation d'exécution ne règle pas), fetch une fois, puis lance le workflow orchestrateur implement-parallel qui fan-out des chaînes indépendantes en parallèle (workflow(implement-lot) en mode worktree, imbrication d'un seul niveau). Nettoyage déterministe du worktree : supprimé après succès (PR créée), conservé et son chemin rapporté après échec pour inspection humaine. Treize agents dédiés (dont rebaser, verifier et relander) et cinq commandes (run, run-parallel, sync, status, reland). Treize agents dédiés : branch-setup (crée la branche de lot depuis la base à jour, arbre propre exigé en mode séquentiel — ou dans un worktree dédié en mode parallèle, arbre principal libre), rebaser (rebase déterministe --onto, préventif et curatif, opère avec git -C en mode worktree), lot-briefer (parse le lot et son mode de vérif, pull les SHALL, détecte le test runner), test-writer (modes TDD/test-after : rouge ou vert selon le mode), test-validator, implementer (tous modes : jusqu'au vert si des tests existent, sinon selon le critère d'acceptation), verifier (modes check/inhérent : vérif observable en contexte frais, ou humanCheckRequired), code-reviewer, review-validator, fix-applier, progress-recorder (coche les Tn/Rn de tasks.md et commite sur la branche dédiée), pr-author (pousse la branche, ouvre la PR/MR gh/glab avec description adaptée au mode, en DRAFT + labels stacked/needs-sync + bloc d'avertissement si la PR est empilée pour l'anti-orphelinage, supprime le worktree en succès) et relander (rattrapage d'un lot orphelin : branche reland depuis le défaut, cherry-pick des commits propres du lot, nouvelle PR ready, commentaire sur l'orpheline). Entièrement autonome : aucune gate humaine en cours de run. Un lancement séquentiel = un lot Rn ; run-parallel lance plusieurs lots indépendants à la fois. L'unité de travail est la vertical slice reviewable produite par scd-feature-specs. Le plugin n'invente aucune techno (agnostique, détectée depuis le projet) et n'écrit aucun document de spec — il honore le contrat et le vérifie, là où scd-feature-specs s'arrête à la gate analyze. Human décide le quoi (le contrat, en amont) ; le workflow exécute le comment et prouve le vert.

entry.title: ai-maestro-assistant-role-agent
entry.content: Role-plugin for ASSISTANT-titled agents in the AI Maestro ecosystem (R39). Each human user (except the MAESTRO) gets exactly one ASSISTANT: it can plan like a MANAGER and program like an AUTONOMOUS agent, but it MUST NOT create agents or teams and has NO governing powers. It serves exactly one user (label 'Assistant of <user>'), obeys ONLY that user (not even the MAESTRO), approves only its OWN TRDDs, has no team, and is invisible to every agent except the MANAGER — which may assign it a refusable task only if the user enabled MANAGER-collaboration (R39.8/R39.9). On a shared GitHub project it acts as a peer with equal authority; when the MANAGER assigns a collaborator there (and the user permits), a scoped, user-revocable channel to that collaborator opens (R39.10). It inherits its user's kanban tasks and granted permissions. Requires AI Maestro for messaging/identity skills.

entry.title: hone
entry.content: hone is a development model where a human writes a short Plan and an automated, worktree-native loop then builds, verifies, consolidates, reviews, and lands each change unattended. Only rot-proof durable truth survives in the repo, and every cycle deletes something. Enforced by a PreToolUse guard, a Stop-hook gate and nag, and two refute-first critics.
